question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed four distinct OPTIONS-style questions at meaningful decision points: scoring strategy, frame-10 handling, validation timing, and an edge-case probe. Nearly every architectural fork was surfaced for the user.

question_quality.rationale: Each question targeted a real design tension: roll() vs score() computation, special-casing frame 10, eager vs lazy validation, and the subtle pins-reset edge case after a 10th-frame strike. These are exactly the cruxes of the bowling problem.

scaffolding.rationale: Progression moves from high-level architecture (where to compute) to structural choice (uniform vs special-case) to validation philosophy, ending with a targeted edge case after the implementation existed. No spoilers; user reasoning was solicited before reveals.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and affirming without flattery, confirmations are concise, and the back-and-forth is fluid. The Pair validates user reasoning then advances naturally; the edge-case probe in turn 4 fits seamlessly.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code passes all manual sanity checks including the tricky 10th-frame edge cases and validation. Comparable to baseline functionally; baseline included a written test suite while experiment used inline checks, but both correctly solve the exercise.

friction.rationale: Experiment took 5 turns / 280s vs baseline's 2 turns / 177s, but the extra length delivered substantive teaching across four design forks rather than just a trace-correction. Proportional pedagogical payoff.
